speaker: interviewer
text: With Irving Rubinstein July 1st, 1995, Chicago, Illinois.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in Camp New Palestine since 1945-- for three years. I was looking for a place, where to go. I tried to go to America. I was unlucky-- I couldn't succeed to get a visa. I tried to go to Australia, I didn't succeed. I found out that my mother had a brother in Canada. I got his address, I wrote him a letter that I'd be interested to come to Canada. I receive an answer from him, saying he cannot make me papers, but I-- I should go to a Jewish agency and tell them that he will sponsor me. I went to Jewish agency, by the name of Joint-- by the name of Joint. And I made an application by them, through their own organization. I made an application, took quite a while. And in 1945-- 1948-- I think early in April-- I got a visa that I can enter Canada. I was very happy to get the visa, but I get-- I couldn't get no transportation to go. Was no ships, the airplanes were full. So I had to wait for next. From Salzburg, I went by train to Bremerhaven, Germany. Took maybe 36 hours by train. When I came there, was no ship, was no plane. I wait there about two or three weeks. From there, I went by train to Holland. I arrived in the Hook of Holland, where I boarded a cattle ship-- or an old warship. And I cross the channel, and I arrived in Hook-- no. No-- no-- no-- I left-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, I left Hook of Holland, and I arrived in Dover-- In Dover, England. Arrived in Dover. From there, they took us by bus to our hotel, and kept us there several weeks. From there, they put us on a plane, and I went to Montreal, Canada-- which took us 21 hours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, I went by train to Toronto, Canada, where I got reunited with my uncle. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Over there, I settled. I went to work. I was always working in the shoe business-- either for myself, or for somebody. Went to work, was struggling hard. Somehow, I was making a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I back up just a little bit? You spent about two and a half years in New Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah,

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during that time, when you were trying to get a visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Palestine, we didn't do too much. There wasn't too much to do. Wasn't too much. Just living a quiet life. You learn a little English, you learn a little religion, a little social work. Worked a little for the camp, but too much we didn't do there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what do you mean by learning a little social work?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, helping others-- go to other countries. Work, this, that, and that. So we killed the time, but there was no work there. You couldn't do nothing. So there was no way of making money?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, was no way of making money. Of course, a foreigner couldn't work. See-- now, yeah, a foreigner cannot work, no. There was no way of earning a dollar, and was no work. So we just killed time, and waited for better days. What was involved in getting the passport? You had to have sponsorship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had the sponsorship-- I had to have a sponsorship, and then the Jewish agency got me the visa. See, usually, the Jewish agency tried to get out the people from Europe. Help them getting out. And wherever you had a relative, it was easier to go. Because they were not responsible. If they Jewish agency found a relative, they were not responsible. You went on your own-- they let you go. They gave you a passport with a visa, and goodbye. Go to your family, and that's all. They got rid of you. Yeah, which was wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With who?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you finally emigrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. By myself, yeah. I went by myself, yeah. I was living in Canada for 10 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After 10 years, I decided I want a change. I want to come to America. I had two brothers in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had trouble getting a visa-- for no reason. I don't know. For unknown reason. But somehow, I was pushing very hard. Until one day, I went to the American Embassy, and I want to find out the reasons why I am refused a visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me, and I appealed, and they believe me, and they gave me a visa. In 1958, in April, they issued me a visa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a lucky year for me-- I bought a new car. I put my belongings in the car, and I supposed to go with my uncle and aunt, to a bas mitzvah, to Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. I took my uncle and aunt in the new car. Ad we drove from Toronto, Canada to Pittsburgh-- was a reunion of the family. We had a bar mitzvah. Over there, I stayed a week. And after that, I took my new car, and came with the tollway from Pittsburgh all the way to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: The visa denial-- you don't need to share if you don't want to, but was there any aspect of retribution for anything you had done?

speaker: interviewee
text: The visa denial was for no reason-- was a mistake, a confusion. Was absolutely-- didn't have nothing to do with me. Was just a confusion with the name spelling. That's all. Otherwise, I'd have got my visa. If I had known before, I could have gone and had it fixed. But they didn't give me the reason. After I got the reason, I went and proved it. I have passport from every move, everything is legal. I never change my name. Since 1922 till today, I have old documents, and everything is by the same name-- never nothing changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they believed me, and they gave me a visa. So after you came here--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here in 1958. I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selling shoes. Stayed here two years. After two years, I made a little money, I was a little successful. I decided to go for vacation, to see the state of Israel. I went to Israel, I came there, I met a cousin of mine. I met a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he introduced me to several girls. And one of the girls, I married and brought her back to America. And I established a new life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1960. The same year, my wife, Renee Rubinstein, gave birth to my first daughter on September. The same year of marriage. And after this, I had two more children. At the present time, I have two married daughters, a son, and five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any connections within your family with survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, connection?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where people have married people who were survivors, or related to survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. My first daughter, married a son of a Holocaust survivor. But there's a funny thing-- they bear the same name as I do, Rubinstein. Seven years ago, my first daughter got married. My in-laws decided that we are both Holocaust survivors, we should go back and visit Poland. Him, his wife, and me and my wife got together, and we went back to Poland. He saw my birthplace, I saw his birthplace. We also went to see the grave of his father, who passed away after the war, in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that being a survivor affected how you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, positively. We mostly associate with Holocaust survivors. We live it from day to day-- by every meeting, by every party, by every gathering, we always remember. And this is always the main subject-- the main discussion is the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that further? What you talk about? What are the important things that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The important thing is that the Holocaust survivors sticks together. We know each other. We meet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We always talk, we always got together. And we always try to remember. We teach our children that the Holocaust should be never forgotten. It should be remembered from generation to generation. Should be remembered, the atrocities, what the Nazis did to the German-- to the Jewish race. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What powers did you discover in yourself as a consequence of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Repeat again?

speaker: interviewer
text: What powers did you discover in yourself as a consequence of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: What powers?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did learn a lot. First of all, I learned about the German race. A respectable, civilized society before 1939-- with the first doctors, with the first lawyers, with the first dentists, the first scientists-- could turn so cruel in a short time. To go and plan to liquidate a race of people for no reason. Only because they were a minority.

speaker: interviewer
text: What lesson do you hope your children learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that my children will remember the Holocaust, and they will live in a better world than what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Better. More freedom, more educated, and live a better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back to when you were, say, eight years old. And you think now. What differences are there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to be eight years today, not 73. I wish I could turn back the clock, to be 34 years longer-- then I would know how to live today. The life would be much better than was so many years ago. The life was never sweet in Europe, and it will be never sweet, either. Europe-- Europe is tough life, especially Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What accounts for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a country which is behind the civilized world. They don't make no progress.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you handle the loss? The losses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard, very hard. By every gathering, by every wedding, by every bar mitzvah, by every birth-- we always remember. And specially when our children are born. We name them after the people, what we lost during the war. Most of my children were-- were named after our ancestors. My first child-- my first grandson was named after the-- our ancestors. The second, and the third, and the fourth, the fifth-- we are named the children of our ancestors. That's the name should be never forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you do other things? That's most profound. Do you do other things? Organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I belong to certain organization. Every year, we have a Holocaust memorial meeting. Once a year, to remember the death. Every several years, I go back to Europe-- to the graves of our ancestors. I've been several times-- in Auschwitz, Treblinka, Majdanek, Sobibor. As a fact, I just returned three weeks ago. On-- on May the 8th, I was at the parade of the 50th year anniversary of the liberation of Europe, when the Germans capitulated. I was there, and I went through all the camps. I want on a tour, and I visited every camp, and lay flowers on every grave. And I hope, I think, this is the last time I went back to Poland. After 50 years, it's time. That's it. This is the last time-- no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: This time it sounds like you went back different than other times you've gone back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did my duty. It was 50 years after the liberation. I said, this is the time to go back, because who knows if we ever return any more. So, that's it. This closes the chapter of the war-- 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But beyond even the official celebration of 50 years, it sounds like this was more important to you personally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went back-- I went back to see the city of my birth, of my ancestors, you know. I have some grave from my great grandfathers in Kielce, so I visited the whole thing. I said, before the person goes, he has to go back and see from where he come. You got to see your roots, so I went to see my roots.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, wonderful. To walk around in Poland as a free man was real great and nice. I was very proud of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing special, no. Nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Rubinstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome, no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my father, which was taken in that group of all the business people in my city in 1932-- at our business. This picture, I didn't have. I found this picture in Canada in 1948. And out of the group, this picture was taken out and was blown up. So this is the only picture I got. This is my father. His name is Leon Rubinstein. He was born in 1988 in Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture. My name is Irving Rubinstein. I was born 8-15-1922, in Kielce, Poland. This picture was taken in 1945, in Salzburg, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we're rolling. Go ahead, Freddy.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture-- this is my picture, Irving Rubinstein. The picture was taken in 1932, in Kielce, Poland. I went to a private school by the name of Tushia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my passport. It was issued to me in 1948-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sometime in March or April. And the signature is-- United Restitution Organization, or short, URO.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken by myself, sometime in 1982, standing behind the gates of Auschwitz, near my barrack, where I used to live. This is the picture of the entrance to Auschwitz, which reads, 'Arbeit Macht Frei', which means, work makes free. Thousands of people, hundred thousands of people went through this gate. Only a handful returned alive from this gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my wife [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] standing on the-- standing on the ramp, on the ramp where they used to unload the Jews on the way to the crematorium. This is a picture of me and my wife celebrating the 50th anniversary of the liberation from the camps. It's a gorgeous picture, a beauty.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left to the right-- my daughter Judy, my son-in-law Sam, and my son Steven. From the left to the right-- my wife Renee, my son Steven, and myself, Irving.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at my daughter's wedding three years ago. Myself, my wife, my daughter Judy, and my son Steven.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest daughter, Ruth, with her husband, Jay, and the four grandchildren-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Devorah, [BACKGROUND NOISE] Yossi, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Moeshe, the other one I've forgotten. I forgot the name-- OK-- Moeshe and Yossi. Got it.
